stories.title: Podcast: Building an AI-Powered CRE Operation – Boxer Property President Justin Segal
stories.excerpt: Justin Segal is President of Boxer Property, where he oversees operations, leasing, technology, and marketing across a national portfolio of office, retail, and hotel assets. He co-founded Brava Systems, an AI-enabled platform originally built for Boxer’s own operations and now deployed across the commercial real estate industry, and Relay Human Cloud, a global staffing and team augmentation company. Justin is based in Houston. Learn more: –Visit Boxer Property –Visit Brava –Justin Segal on LinkedIn NYC Real Estate Week: -CTO Exchange & Hackathon: 3-in-1 event on Oct. 19 featuring C-suite roundtables, a hackathon and the Power AI & Tech Awards reception (invite-only). Email ecohen@commercialobserver.com for info. CRE Events coming up: –Institutional Investors & PE Forum: in NYC on 9/16 –South Florida Capital & Development Forum: in Miami on 10/15 –All upcoming events Connect with Tangent: -Edward Cohen on LinkedIn -Zach Aarons on LinkedIn -Tangent on LinkedInDisclaimer: Commercial Observer Tangent podcast is for entertainment and informational purposes only, you should not construe any such information or other material as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice. Nothing contained on our program constitutes a solicitation, recommendation, endorsement, or offer by Tangent, Commercial Observer, MetaProp, or any third party guest to buy or sell any securities, public or private, other financial instruments or funds.
bullets: Boxer Property President Justin Segal leads AI-enabled CRE operations platform across national office, retail, hotel portfolio.
Brava Systems AI platform deployed across commercial real estate industry for operations optimization.
No direct multifamily development impact; primarily office, retail, and hospitality focus.
stories.summary: Justin Segal of Boxer Property oversees a national portfolio using AI-powered operations and technology platform (Brava Systems), which he co-founded and has scaled across the CRE industry. The platform addresses operational efficiency in commercial real estate. This is a technology infrastructure story with limited direct relevance to multifamily development.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Technology efficiency gains in property operations may marginally reduce operating costs across asset classes, but the article focuses on office, retail, and hospitality—not residential.

stories.title: Mamdani Appoints Lina Khan as Board Chair of EDC
stories.excerpt: In a flurry of announcements on Wednesday, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani has appointed Lina Khan, the former chair of the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), as the new board chair of New York City’s Economic Development Corporation (EDC). Khan, also a progressive antitrust regulator, will lead the EDC’s 27-member board and help guide the city’s major development projects. Khan was the youngest-ever chair of the FTC during the Biden administration, when she led an aggressive shift in antitrust policy and spearheaded lawsuits against major tech conglomerates and targeted large corporate mergers to reduce consumer costs. “Lina Khan has fought exploitation and dishonesty in courtrooms and classrooms across the country, and given us the blueprint for a government that delivers for working people. As chair of the EDC’s board of directors, I know that Lina will not only steer our administration’s economic development efforts toward a new era of opportunity, but ensure justice is the driving principle behind all of our work,” Mamdani said in a statement on Wednesday. In her new role as board chair of the EDC, Khan will succeed Margaret Anadu, who was appointed by then-Mayor Eric Adams in 2022. Khan’s appointment is separate from the appointment of Anthony Shorris, who Mamdani named Wednesday as the new president of the EDC. Shorris will run the EDC’s day-to-day operations as the city’s primary vehicle for development and infrastructure growth, and oversee projects such as five new city-run grocery stores. Meanwhile, Khan will guide the overarching policy of the EDC and approve its projects. “As Tony shepherds the EDC into a new moment of economic innovation, including the implementation of five city-run grocery stores, one in each borough, I know he and Lina will accomplish a great deal together,” Mamdani said in an announcement, according to Crain’s New York Business. “Frankly, this feels like the economic wonk’s version of the 1992 Olympic basketball dream team.”
bullets: NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani appoints Lina Khan (former FTC chair) as NYC EDC board chair.
Khan led aggressive antitrust enforcement against tech mergers and large corporate consolidation.
Progressive regulatory stance signals potential scrutiny of large real estate deals, land assemblies, and institutional investor concentration in NYC market.
stories.summary: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani named Lina Khan, the Biden administration's FTC chair known for aggressive antitrust enforcement and opposition to corporate consolidation, as chair of NYC's Economic Development Corporation (EDC). Khan will oversee the EDC's 27-member board and guide the city's major development projects. Her appointment signals a shift toward scrutiny of market consolidation and corporate power.
stories.impact: Khan's antitrust philosophy and consumer-protection focus could reshape NYC development policy, potentially blocking large institutional acquisitions, scrutinizing land assemblies by major developers, and favoring smaller or minority-owned developers. This may slow consolidation but also slow capital flow into NYC multifamily development, complicating financing and acquisition paths for large players like TCR; smaller developers may face regulatory favor but capital constraints.

stories.title: AI Job Displacement Risk, AI Office Demand Dovetail
stories.excerpt: U.S. markets defined by sectors most exposed to AI-driven job displacement are also seeing the strongest real estate demand from AI companies, according to new research from JLL. Conducted in partnership with MIT’s Sloan School of Management and Center for Real Estate, the research shows that AI is creating deep divergence across markets, industries, and asset classes, separating those with the capacity to adapt from those without. JLL’s Where AI is Changing Jobs and What it Means for Real Estate research finds that even as overall U.S. tech employment declined by 1.5% in early 2026, office leasing demand in the sector continues to rebound. There’s a clear decoupling of AI growth from broader trends in tech and other office-using industries, according to JLL. In San Francisco, for example, nearly 30% of total leasing since 2025 has come from AI companies, while the city carries among the highest exposure to AI-driven job dislocation risks in the U.S. JLL determined that a market’s capacity to adapt, capitalize on new opportunities and redeploy the workforce is more critical to real estate performance than exposure risk alone. “The winning real estate strategies will be those that look beyond the headlines about job losses and focus on a market and industry’s ability to adapt,” said Alexandra Bryant, global CEO, Value & Risk Advisory at JLL. “It’s no longer about whether a market has AI exposure. It’s about whether it has the right mix of talent, infrastructure, and quality real estate to capitalize on the opportunities AI creates.” The post AI Job Displacement Risk, AI Office Demand Dovetail appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: San Francisco: 30% of office leasing since 2025 from AI companies despite high AI job-displacement exposure.
Overall US tech employment down 1.5% early 2026; AI sector leasing demand decouples and continues rebounding.
Market's capacity to adapt—talent, infrastructure, real estate quality—more critical to RE performance than AI displacement risk alone.
stories.summary: JLL research (with MIT Sloan and Center for Real Estate) finds that U.S. markets with highest AI job-displacement risk are simultaneously attracting strongest AI company office leasing demand. San Francisco exemplifies this: nearly 30% of office leasing since 2025 came from AI companies. Overall tech employment declined 1.5% in early 2026, yet AI leasing rebounded. Markets with adaptive capacity (workforce redeployment, talent pool, quality real estate) outperform those with displacement risk alone.
stories.impact: AI-driven polarization is reshaping talent geography and office demand in ways that indirectly lift multifamily demand in AI hubs (San Francisco, Boston, NYC tech corridors) while depressing it in legacy tech markets. A developer's residential pipeline should target metros with strong AI adaptation signals—those attracting AI-company expansion—rather than assume broad tech sector decline. This favors core tech metros over secondary markets dependent on displaced white-collar workers; multifamily fundamentals in adaptation-capable metros should strengthen despite aggregate tech job losses.

stories.title: Dallas Apartment Builder Inks $78.7M Refi
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged a $78.7 million construction takeout financing for The Flynn at Live Oak, a five-story, 327-unit newly completed luxury multifamily community in Dallas, Texas. JLL represented the borrower, Conor Commercial Real Estate and Globe Corporation, in securing the loan through funds managed by Blue Owl Capital Inc. Located at 4931 Live Oak St. The Flynn at Live Oak features a mix of studios, one- and two-bedroom apartments averaging 832 square feet. Residents enjoy amenities such as a 2,500-square-foot pool with sun shelf, two outdoor courtyards with fire pits and grilling stations, a sky lounge with downtown views, a fitness center with outdoor yoga space, co-working offices and a grab-and-go convenience store. The property also provides 420 parking spaces with electric vehicle charging stations on every garage level. JLL Capital Market’s Debt Advisory team, representing the borrower, was led by Lucas Borges, Lauren Dow, Sam Tarter, Aidan Flanagan and Christian Johnston. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Dallas Apartment Builder Inks $78.7M Refi appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: The Flynn at Live Oak, Dallas: 327-unit luxury community completed; $78.7M construction takeout via Blue Owl Capital.
Strong financing execution on newly delivered product in Dallas; 420 parking spaces with EV charging on every level.
Competitive Dallas multifamily deliveries continuing to lease and refinance into strong debt markets; cap rate recovery signals investor confidence.
stories.summary: JLL Capital Markets arranged a $78.7 million construction takeout refinance for The Flynn at Live Oak, a 327-unit luxury multifamily community in Dallas completed in 2024. Blue Owl Capital provided the financing. The property features studios, one- and two-bedroom units (avg 832 sf) with premium amenities including rooftop lounge, co-working, and EV charging on all garage levels. Strong execution on completed asset refinance signals healthy Dallas multifamily market absorption.
stories.impact: Dallas multifamily continues to pull strong permanent financing on newly delivered luxury product, indicating solid absorption and lender confidence in market fundamentals. The EV charging infrastructure (every garage level) reflects rising resident demand and developer differentiation in competitive luxury markets. This signals Dallas remains a target market for additional supply and validates further development starts; cost of debt remains favorable for well-leased assets, supporting continued starts and expansions in the metro.

stories.title: Is the Market Mispricing the Rate Hike Probability?
stories.excerpt: Executive Summary Inflation expectations have fallen sharply following last week’s benign CPI and PPI prints, yet the market continues to price a meaningful probability of an interest rate hike by the Federal Reserve — a disconnect that appears mispriced. On balance, the Fed will likely look through the near-term energy spike and hold rates steady for an extended period. The front end of the U.S. curve looks rich, particularly at five years, and is increasingly inconsistent with a rate-hike cycle. A Structural Shift A fundamental structural shift is taking hold across the fixed-income landscape, challenging investors to reposition for a classic dual-ended yield curve steepening. While financial markets have historically attributed yield fluctuations to near-term business cycles, the current elevation in long-dated real rates reflects a deeper macroeconomic evolution tied to the tech-driven productivity revolution. This shift places structural upward pressure on the back end of the curve, keeping real rates well above the ultra-low regimes observed during the post-Global Financial Crisis and pandemic eras. Concurrently, a dramatic cooling in recent consumer price index (CPI) and producer price index (PPI) prints has somewhat neutralized fears of monetary tightening, carving a clear path for short duration yields to recede. Decoupling Inflation Expectations from Fed Narrative The juxtaposition between calming inflation expectations and lingering market anxieties over high interest rates reveals a distinct mispricing in interest rate futures. Recent friendly wholesale and consumer inflation metrics indicate that the broader market has discounted the transitory spikes seen in headline data. While a restrictive monetary stance remains a close call, the Federal Reserve is likely to look through localized energy price shocks, opting instead to maintain the federal funds rate at current levels before eventually executing a pivot. This macro backdrop removes the immediate th
bullets: Recent CPI and PPI prints benign; market pricing meaningful rate-hike probability despite falling inflation expectations—apparent mispricing.
Fed likely to hold rates steady; front end of yield curve (5-year) looks rich and inconsistent with rate-hike cycle.
Tech-driven productivity revolution creating structural upward pressure on long-dated real rates; yield curve steepening expected.
stories.summary: Recent benign CPI and PPI data have caused inflation expectations to fall sharply, yet financial markets continue to price meaningful probability of a Fed rate hike—a potential mispricing. Analysis concludes the Fed will likely hold rates steady for an extended period and suggests the front end of the yield curve (especially 5-year) is overpriced relative to fundamentals. A structural shift in fixed-income landscape driven by tech-productivity gains keeps long-dated real rates elevated above post-GFC and pandemic lows, setting up a classic curve steepening.
stories.impact: If rates remain steady or decline (as analysis suggests), acquisition and construction financing costs should stabilize or improve, supporting underwriting on new multifamily starts. However, structural elevation in long-dated rates means CMBS and long-duration debt will remain expensive; developers should favor shorter-duration construction loans and rate locks. The curve steepening thesis implies refinance risk for long-dated 10-year+ debt but opportunity in short-duration construction vehicles—practical implication: lock in 3–5 year construction financing before potential curve flattening.

stories.title: Blackstone Lands $205M Refi for Brickell Hotel
stories.excerpt: JPMorgan Chase has lent Blackstone $205 million to refinance East Miami, the 352-room hotel. EAST Miami is a 352-key full-service hotel in Miami’s Brickell neighborhood. Blackstone purchased the property in 2025 for $300 million. Trinity Investments and Certares Real Estate Management were the sellers. The 39-story tower is located at 788 Brickell Plaza. The hotel opened in 2016 as part of the first phase of Brickell City Centre, a $1.1 billion, 5 million-square-foot complex developed by Swire Properties. Designed by Arquitectonica, EAST Miami includes 255 rooms, 89 residences and eight suites with floor-to-ceiling windows and private balconies. Amenities feature a rooftop bar dubbed Sugar, a swimming pool with a bar and lounge spaces, a signature restaurant, a fitness center, wellness facilities, a tearoom and the Domain, a part café, part coworking space at the property’s lobby-level. Additionally, the 536,070-square-foot building includes multiple conference rooms and meeting venues.  The post Blackstone Lands $205M Refi for Brickell Hotel appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Blackstone: $205M refi for EAST Miami, 352-room hotel; JPMorgan Chase lender. Purchased property in 2025 for $300M.
Hotel opened 2016; part of Swire Properties' $1.1B Brickell City Centre mixed-use complex with 89 residential units in same tower.
Brickell hospitality asset refinancing confirms strong debt availability; hotel/residential mixed-use model in Miami core market performing well.
stories.summary: JPMorgan Chase provided Blackstone a $205 million refinance for EAST Miami, a 352-room, 39-story hotel in Miami's Brickell neighborhood that Blackstone acquired in 2025 for $300 million. The property includes 89 residential units and opened in 2016 as part of Swire Properties' $1.1 billion Brickell City Centre. Strong permanent financing on hospitality asset signals robust debt availability and confidence in Miami's core market performance.
stories.impact: Hotel refi execution in Miami confirms hospitality debt markets remain active despite broader market volatility. The mixed-use model (hotel + residential + retail) in Brickell shows successful asset class blending in prime CBD locations. For multifamily developers, this signals Brickell/Miami CBD remains investable, financing is available, and hotel-anchored mixed-use offers liquidity—relevant if considering joint-venture or mixed-use development in Miami core markets.

stories.title: Mamdani Expected to Name Government Vet Anthony Shorris to Lead EDC
stories.excerpt: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani is expected to name Anthony Shorris, a former first deputy mayor in the Bill de Blasio administration, to lead the New York City Economic Development Corporation (EDC). Several reports suggest that the announcement could come Wednesday, as the mayor looks to fill a seat that has been vacant for several months. “We congratulate Anthony Shorris on his appointment to lead NYCEDC. Tony is a thoughtful, experienced and effective leader with a deep understanding of New York City’s economy and the challenges facing businesses and communities across the five boroughs,” James Whelan, president of the Real Estate Board of New York, said in a statement on Wednesday. “At a time when attracting investment and creating jobs is more important than ever, his leadership will be an asset to the city.” Shorris and the EDC did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. Shorris, who is currently a partner with global consulting firm McKinsey & Company, is a government veteran, having served as the executive director of the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, as well as deputy chancellor of the New York City public school system, in addition to his role with the de Blasio administration. “This will be the person I turn to to run the day-to-day operations of government,” de Blasio once said of Shorris. Shorris’s possible appointment to the top seat at the EDC might be the mayor’s way of appeasing the city’s business leaders, according to The New York Times — which reports having spoken with several sources close to the hiring conversations. When Mandani took office there were concerns he would interfere with the EDC’s efforts to incentivize private capital investment needed to bring more housing to the city and make critical infrastructure improvements. When Mamdani, a staunch democratic socialist, took office at the start of this year, he circulated a memo calling for an overhaul of the EDC
bullets: NYC Mayor Mamdani to appoint Anthony Shorris (ex-deputy mayor, McKinsey partner) as EDC executive director.
Shorris: Port Authority exec, NYC schools deputy chancellor, de Blasio administration operative; appointment signals business-friendly governance pivot.
Appointment may appease NYC business leaders after Khan's aggressive antitrust stance; operational experience in government infrastructure key.
stories.summary: NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani is expected to name Anthony Shorris, a McKinsey partner and government veteran (former first deputy mayor under de Blasio, Port Authority exec, NYC schools deputy chancellor), as executive director of the NYC Economic Development Corporation. The appointment follows Khan's naming as EDC board chair and is seen as an effort to reassure the city's business community while bringing operational expertise in government and infrastructure.
stories.impact: Shorris's appointment as EDC executive director alongside Khan as board chair creates a hybrid governance model: Khan (antitrust hawk, policy direction) + Shorris (operations, business relationships, government execution). This may accelerate approval of large mixed-use developments and land deals that navigate regulatory scrutiny—Shorris's Port Authority and schools experience suggests comfort with infrastructure bundling and public-private partnerships. For TCR and similar developers, Shorris may smooth entitlements and financing on complex NYC projects, though Khan's board presence will maintain baseline consumer/worker protection scrutiny. Net effect: faster project movement, but with tighter regulatory guardrails.

stories.title: Publix-Anchored Jax Retail Center Changes Hands
stories.excerpt: TSCG brokered the sale of The Plaza at Normandy, a newly developed, Publix-anchored shopping center in Jacksonville, Florida, for $20.6 million. Located at the intersection of Normandy Boulevard and Chaﬀee Road South, the fully leased center was sold on behalf of Wagner Property Group to a 1031 exchange buyer. Originally built in 2024, The Plaza at Normandy totals 58,691 square feet and was 100% leased at the time of sale. Publix occupies 46,791 square feet in its newest prototype grocery store, featuring a drive-thru pharmacy and an adjacent 2,100-square-foot space leased to a liquor store. Other tenants include Xtreme Wings, Hair Cucery, Nail Art Studio & Spa, UPS Store and AT&T Wireless. Separately owned outparcels with McDonald’s and Wawa were not a part of the transaction. The TSCG investment sales team included Anthony Blanco, Mallory Silva, and Spencer Kinsella. The buyer was represented by Laurie Ann Drinkwater of Marcus & Millichap. The post Publix-Anchored Jax Retail Center Changes Hands appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: The Plaza at Normandy, Jacksonville, FL: Publix-anchored center, 58,691 sf, fully leased; sold for $20.6M (built 2024).
Publix 46,791 sf anchor; outparcels (McDonald's, Wawa) separately owned and not included in sale.
New grocery-anchored retail center in secondary market liquidity; Publix prototype with drive-thru pharmacy and liquor tenant shows modern grocery format demand.
stories.summary: TSCG brokered the sale of The Plaza at Normandy, a newly built (2024), fully leased Publix-anchored shopping center in Jacksonville, Florida, for $20.6 million. The 58,691-square-foot center includes Publix (46,791 sf with drive-thru pharmacy), a liquor store, and tenants like Xtreme Wings, Hair Cucery, and UPS Store. Separate outparcels with McDonald's and Wawa were not part of the transaction. Strong execution on newly built, fully leased center reflects healthy retail demand in Jacksonville secondary market.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Retail center sale signals healthy Jacksonville retail fundamentals and confidence in secondary-market convenience retail. Indirectly, if retail cap rates remain compressed (20.6M on fully leased, new center suggests strong pricing), it may indicate investor capital flowing into secondary markets and lower discount rates available for mixed-use deals including residential components in Jacksonville—relevant if considering mixed-use projects in that market.

stories.title: The True Costs of New York’s Rent Freeze Will Make Themselves Known
stories.excerpt: The New York City Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) recently voted to freeze rents on both one- and two-year rent-stabilized lease renewals, fulfilling one of Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s signature campaign promises. For the approximately 2 million New Yorkers living in rent-stabilized apartments, the decision was understandably welcomed as a financial reprieve during a time when the cost of living continues to rise. The question, however, is: at what cost? When political rhetoric and the constant noise of modern media are set aside, housing policy should be evaluated through a clear and objective lens. Regardless of political affiliation, effective policy must be measured by its real-world impact: whether the intended benefits outweigh unintended consequences, whether it addresses the root cause of the problem, and whether it is supported by sound economic principles. Michael J. Romer Photo: Courtesy Romer Debbas Applying those questions to rent freezes reveals a more complicated picture. The New York multifamily landscape changed dramatically in 2019 with the passing of the Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act of 2019 (HSTPA). Much to landlords’ dismay, the HSTPA expanded tenant protections, limited rent increases associated with building improvements and effectively ended the pathway to apartment deregulation where market-rate rents could be charged. Since the passing of HSTPA, multifamily landlords have faced operational challenges and some of the prominent banks that financed these properties have disappeared. Even today, numerous banks remain burdened by high-risk multifamily loans, as declining property values have left many properties worth less than the debt they secure. There is no doubt that freezing rent increases provides much-needed relief to tenants that face the ever-increasing costs to live in New York City. However, this relief comes at a significant cost, impacting both the property owners and tenants receiving relief. According to the RGB Price Index
bullets: NYC Rent Guidelines Board votes to freeze rents on 1- and 2-year rent-stabilized lease renewals; affects ~2M New Yorkers.
Freeze fulfills Mayor Mamdani campaign promise; first rent freeze since 2022; signals expanding tenant-protection regime.
Article flags unintended consequences: reduced landlord capital, deferred maintenance, lower development incentives—structural headwinds for multifamily supply.
stories.summary: The NYC Rent Guidelines Board approved a rent freeze on both one- and two-year rent-stabilized lease renewals, affecting approximately 2 million New Yorkers and fulfilling Mayor Mamdani's campaign pledge. The article emphasizes that while rent freezes provide tenant relief, they carry unintended economic consequences: reduced landlord revenue, deferred maintenance incentives, and diminished new-construction incentives—particularly relevant after HSTPA (2019) already expanded tenant protections and compressed landlord economics.
stories.impact: NYC rent freeze on stabilized units (a core asset for many multifamily owners and refinancing pools) signals further compression of landlord returns and accelerated shift toward market-rate new construction. Combined with HSTPA, frozen stabilized rents will depress valuations on rent-stabilized portfolios, tightening refinance options and discouraging preservation/conversion projects. For TCR and peers, this pushes incentives toward: (1) market-rate new construction in high-barrier neighborhoods (Manhattan core), (2) sale of stabilized portfolios to value-add investors seeking tax loss harvesting, (3) exit from rent-stabilized management. Supply response will likely further bifurcate NYC: minimal new stabilized units, accelerated market-rate development targeting high-income renters, and eventual underinvestment in existing stabilized stock.

stories.title: NYC’s Conversion Break Producing Far More Units Than New-Construction Sweetener
stories.excerpt: It’s been well documented and much lamented just how few ground-up residential units are being developed and constructed in New York City due to the limitations of 485-x, a 2-year-old state tax incentive that appears to guide most developers to a 99-unit limit on new multifamily buildings in much of the city in order for them to avoid paying construction wage minimums of $40 an hour. This becomes especially stark when compared to the robust productivity of 467-m, another 2-year-old state tax break, this one for office-to-residential conversions and without any wage requirements. Now, one enterprising industry professional has put the comparison in stark terms, and it doesn’t bode well for 485-x. According to Nate Bliss, founder and principal of Latent Urban Ventures and a former chief of staff to the deputy mayor for housing in Eric Adams’s administration, the average office-to-residential conversion project in the city — including completed and planned projects under 467-m — produces 298 apartments. By comparison, the average new construction project under 485-x creates a grand total of just 39.4 apartments per project. “The problems with the 485-x program were well known at the time it passed,” said Bliss. “The biggest surprise to me is just how much has been generated under the 467-m program. If you look at this first wave of projects, we’ve got over twice as many permanently affordable apartments delivered with 467-m as we do with 485-x.” Bliss, who utilized pipeline data — meaning that his numbers included both projects that are underway and projects in the application stage — found that there are currently 55 conversions filed or underway under 467-m, with another 15 in active plan review. In total, these projects are slated to produce up to 20,876 apartments, 5,219 of them potentially designated permanently affordable. As for new construction under 485-x, he found 301 prospective registrations for a total of 11,869 intended apartments, with 2,557 afford
bullets: NYC 467-m (office-to-residential conversion): avg 298 units per project; no wage requirements; robust supply pipeline.
NYC 485-x (new construction): avg 99 units to avoid $40/hr construction wage mandate; weak new-supply incentive.
Conversion strategy outpacing new construction 3:1 by unit count; structural shift away from ground-up development in NYC.
stories.summary: NYC's 467-m office-to-residential conversion tax incentive is producing far more units than 485-x new-construction incentive. Average conversion projects yield 298 apartments; average new-construction projects yield 99 units (developers cap at 99 to avoid $40/hour construction wage requirement). According to Nate Bliss (Latent Urban Ventures, ex-deputy mayor housing), conversions are dramatically outpacing new construction as a supply strategy, signaling structural preference for adaptive reuse over ground-up development.
stories.impact: The 3:1 unit-productivity gap between 467-m (conversions) and 485-x (new construction) reveals NYC's supply strategy is now anchored to existing office stock rather than greenfield development. This has two major implications: (1) new multifamily supply in NYC will be capped by available office stock (finite) rather than enabled by zoning/incentives (elastic), slowing long-term housing growth; (2) land and entitlement value for new construction sites is collapsing as conversions dominate pipeline, reducing speculative acquisitions and making ground-up development non-viable for TCR and peers without anchor tenants. Developers should pivot to office-to-residential conversion partnerships or exit NYC new-construction market for stable secondary markets; the era of ground-up NYC multifamily development is contracting.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami mansion sale: $18.9M for newly built 10,000-sf home in Ponce-Davis; telecom executive buyer.
Wynwood commercial sale: $24.5M for 13,000-sf buildings; Moishe Mana buyer (developer/investor).
High-end residential and commercial transaction activity in Miami; Mana portfolio expansion in Wynwood creative district.
stories.summary: South Florida residential and commercial transaction report highlights a $18.9 million sale of a newly built mansion in Miami's Ponce-Davis neighborhood to a telecom executive, and a $24.5 million sale of Wynwood buildings to Moishe Mana (developer/investor). Transactions demonstrate active high-net-worth residential market and continued commercial investment in Miami creative/cultural districts. No direct multifamily implications.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Luxury residential and boutique commercial sales reflect Miami's continued appeal to high-income residents and investors; Mana's ongoing Wynwood expansion suggests continued mixed-use/cultural-amenity development in South Florida creative districts. Indirectly, strong luxury residential pricing and investor interest in Miami creative districts may support multifamily rent growth and investor appetite for Miami-area student/young-professional housing adjacent to Wynwood; however, these are primarily luxury/lifestyle transactions outside the multifamily mainstream.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing + Capstone Equities: 316-unit 100% affordable development (The Green at Warner Center, Woodland Hills, LA).
Project qualifies for expedited approval under LA Executive Directive 1; part of larger Warner Center affordable-housing cluster.
Stan Kroenke's 52-acre mixed-use Rams HQ development (Warner Center) anchoring regional multifamily demand and affordable housing policy.
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street in Woodland Hills, Los Angeles, and plan a 316-unit, 100% affordable housing development (The Green at Warner Center) across seven stories on 2.7 acres. The project qualifies for expedited approval under LA's Executive Directive 1 (affordable housing fast-track). This follows a similar Elysian-Capstone conversion project in the San Fernando Valley and joins a broader affordable-housing cluster in Warner Center as Stan Kroenke develops his 52-acre mixed-use center around the Rams headquarters.
stories.impact: Warner Center is emerging as a major affordable-housing policy hub in LA, driven by Kroenke's $1B+ mixed-use investment and city incentives (ED-1 expedited approval). For TCR and peers, this signals LA is directing multifamily supply toward 100% affordable projects in submarkets with strong mixed-use/employment anchors (Kroenke Rams HQ). Opportunity: market-rate multifamily in Warner Center and nearby San Fernando Valley submarkets may benefit from workforce absorption from affordable units + Rams HQ job growth; developers should layer market-rate projects adjacent to affordable-focused initiatives to capture spillover demand. Policy risk: continued shift toward 100% affordable production reduces for-profit multifamily development economics in LA; TCR should prioritize markets with balanced incentive structures (mixed-income, not 100% affordable-only).

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, TX (Austin suburb): selected Griffin Swinerton (SF-based JV) to revamp downtown; target start 2028.
Liberty Hill: one of fastest-growing US suburbs; population tripled since 2020 census; masterplan adopted 2024.
Griffin Swinerton also developing 29-acre Downtown East in Pflugerville and 15-acre downtown project in different Austin suburb.
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, Texas (Austin suburb), selected Griffin Swinerton, a San Francisco-based joint venture, to lead its downtown revitalization project. Liberty Hill has tripled in size since 2020 and adopted a masterplan in 2024. Griffin Swinerton will solicit community input, evaluate infrastructure needs, and guide market financing strategy; groundbreaking targeted for 2028. The firm is also managing similar projects in Pflugerville (29-acre Downtown East) and another Austin suburb.
stories.impact: Liberty Hill's explosive growth (3x since 2020) and outsourced downtown redevelopment strategy signals Texas suburban markets are now at saturation density requiring master-planned town centers to absorb continued in-migration. For TCR, this represents an underrated opportunity: suburban Austin submarkets (Liberty Hill, Pflugerville, etc.) are in early phases of downtown activation and will see concentrated multifamily demand tied to town-center mixed-use projects. Developers should acquire adjacent residential sites in these fast-growth suburbs NOW before Griffin Swinerton's downtown projects drive land values up; suburban multifamily absorbed into town-center ecosystems will command premium rents relative to highway-corridor sprawl. This is a 3–5 year pre-development play in Austin's outer ring.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies: 910 South Wells (Chicago South Loop) secures piles permit; 28-story, 350-unit mixed-use tower underway.
CMK secured $83M construction loan from Wintrust Bank; Gensler design; Brandt's Build general contractor.
Riverline Parcel F Building B (910 South Wells) is part of CMK's 8-acre master plan after Lendlease partnership split in 2018.
stories.summary: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells development in Chicago's South Loop has secured its piles permit, clearing the path for foundation work. The 28-story mixed-use tower (designed by Gensler) will include 350 residential units, 3,300 square feet of retail, and extensive parking. CMK also secured an $83 million construction loan from Wintrust Bank. The project is part of CMK's truncated Riverline master plan (8 acres) following the 2018 split with Lendlease on the original 15-acre vision.
stories.impact: CMK's momentum on Riverline—piles permit for 910 South Wells plus active construction at 1010 South Wells (388 units)—indicates Chicago South Loop multifamily pipeline remains robust despite Midwest regional softness. Strong construction financing (Wintrust $83M) and rapid permitting signal Chicago lenders remain confident in riverfront mixed-use development. For TCR, South Loop represents a proven mixed-use model with high barriers to entry (entitlements, riverfront access, union labor). If seeking Chicago growth, look to secondary corridors or north-side neighborhoods where entitlement risk is lower; South Loop is now dominance territory for CMK and peers with established riverfront positions.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas: $800M wellness resort on 24-acre Trinity River site; 2027 groundbreaking target; adding skybridge to design.
New York Times exposé: Therme misrepresented experience; claimed six global facilities under construction; only one (Bucharest) built.
Dallas approval pending for new design elements; credibility questions linger on developer execution capacity despite glamorous project rendering.
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness company, is seeking city approval for design modifications (skybridge, non-residential zones) to its $800 million Trinity River resort development. The 500,000-square-foot, 24-acre project targets 2027 groundbreaking. However, a New York Times investigation revealed Therme misrepresented its development experience to Ontario (and implicitly Dallas officials), claiming six globally placed facilities under construction when only one (Bucharest spa) had been completed by Therme itself.
stories.impact: Therme Dallas poses significant execution risk for the Dallas market and reveals credibility gaps in international developer track records. While the project design is compelling (thermal baths, beach-like atmosphere, Trinity River waterfront activation), the developer's proven capacity is questionable. For TCR and Dallas competitors: (1) Therme's delays or failure would leave a prime 24-acre Trinity River site vacant for years, depressing waterfront-adjacent multifamily comps; (2) if Therme succeeds, it becomes a major employment and amenity anchor, driving multifamily demand in downtown/Trinity River submarkets—proceed cautiously on nearby residential development until Therme achieves substantial completion and revenue clarity; (3) use Therme's credibility issues to negotiate better terms if acquiring or partnering on sites near the resort.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development: $28M mid-construction financing for The Albritton (Friendswood, TX mixed-use); S3 Capital lender.
The Albritton: 111 multifamily units + 11,000 sf retail + 9,000 sf office; completion 2028; 70% LTV financing.
Tannos also developing Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106-acre mixed-use district) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200-acre).
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28 million in mid-construction financing from S3 Capital for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas (25 miles southeast of Houston). The property will include 111 multifamily units, 11,000 square feet of retail, and 9,000 square feet of office space, targeting completion in 2028. Financing has 70% loan-to-value ratio. Tannos is simultaneously developing Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106-acre mixed-use) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200-acre).
stories.impact: Tannos's multi-project scale in Houston exurbs (Friendswood and Magnolia) signals confidence in suburban Houston absorption and mixed-use economics, but also reveals capital intensity: three major projects totaling $1.75B+ require disciplined execution and sustained financing availability. For TCR: (1) Friendswood and Magnolia are emerging as primary growth vectors for Houston multifamily, driven by mixed-use master-planning (not highway-corridor sprawl); (2) mid-construction financing at 70% LTV is tight—lenders expect strong lease-up and absorption, indicating market fundamentals are solid but margin-constrained; (3) if Tannos completes Friendswood City Center and Magnolia Town Center on schedule, supply concentration risk in those submarkets is high, likely compressing rents by 2028–2030—monitor closely and consider earlier entry into less-saturated Houston corridors (Woodlands, Katy) to avoid submarket overlap with Tannos pipeline.
